content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

June 6, 1995

Legrand H. Clegg II
City Attorney
City of Compton
205 South Willowbrook Avenue
Compton, California 90220

Re: Your Request For Advice
Our File No. A-95-185

Dear Mr. Clegg:

We respond to your request for advice regarding the honoraria
prohibitions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

Are payments for speeches earned in connection with your
business prohibited honoraria?

CONCLUSION

No. Payments for speeches earned in connection with a bona
fide business or trade are exempt from the honoraria prohibitions
of the Act. Your business qualifies as bona fide under the
applicable Commission regulation and therefore you are not
prohibited from accepting payment for speeches you conduct as part
of your business. All payments which aggregate $250 or more from
a single source would, of course, be subject to disclosure as
income on your annual statement of economic interests.

FACTS

You are the elected city attorney for the City of Compton,
California. You are also the principal owner of The Clegg Series,
a business which produces, markets and distributes educational

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18954.

428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

File No. A-95-185
Page 2


materials. You have operated your business for the past four
years, keeping accounting books and records for that entire
period. Moreover, you have filed business tax returns since 1992.
As part of your business activities, you occasionally deliver
public speeches on the subjects covered by the materials you sell.
You are paid for these speeches.

ANALYSIS

Local elected officials are prohibited from accepting an
honorarium. (Section 89501.) An honorarium is any payment made
in consideration of a speech given or an article published, but
does not include, among other things, earned income for personal
services which are customarily provided in connection with the
practice of a bona fide business, unless the sole or predominant
activity of that business is making speeches. (Section 89502.)

Under Commission Regulation 18932.1, a business is presumed
to be "bona fide," within the meaning of Section 89502, if the
owner of the business has maintained books and records, and filed
tax returns, consistent with the operation of that business, for
two calendar years immediately preceding the year in which the
payment, otherwise subject to the honoraria ban, is received.

As a local elected official, you are subject to the honoraria
prohibition of Section 89501 of the Act. However, the payments
for speeches that you receive are clearly earned income in
connection with your business activity. Since your business has
kept adequate records over a period of at least two years, it
qualifies as a bona fide business activity under the applicable
Commission regulation. Therefore, since the sole or predominant
activity of your business is not making speeches, but rather
producing educational materials, the occasional payments you
receive in consideration for public speaking regarding the
educational programs you produce are not prohibited honoraria.2

Please note that these payments are considered income under
the Act and must be reported on your statement of economic
interests if payments aggregating $250 or more are received from
any one source. Additionally, such income may disqualify you from
making, participating in making, or otherwise attempting to
influence a governmental decision which may financially affect
that source of that income. (Sections 87100, 87103.)


2      Speechmaking constitutes a predominant business activity when
more than fifty percent of your business activities in the prior
twelve months were dedicated to the preparation and/or delivery of
speeches; or, if more than fifty percent of your business income
is derived from giving speeches. (Regulation 18932.3.) If under
the above two tests, speechmaking is considered the predominant
activity of your business, the payments would be considered
prohibited honoraria.

File No. A-95-185
Page 3


We trust that this letter adequately addresses the points
raised in your request.  Should you need any additional
information regarding this advice, please contact the undersigned
at (916) 322-5660.


                                        Sincerely,

                                        Steven G. Churchwell
                                        General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        By:  Daniel E. Muallem
                                             Counsel, Legal Division
